title: A Closer Look at Emerging Market Resilience During Recent Shocks

source.name: New York Fed Liberty Street Economics

summary: New York Fed researchers analyze the divergent resilience of emerging market economies during recent global shocks, finding that "Core" EMs (22 MSCI Index countries) have substantially stronger buffers and better outcomes than "Periphery" EMs (92 countries outside the index). Core EMs share of global GDP doubled from 14% to 33% over 25 years, while Periphery EMs remained flat at 6%. During risk-off episodes, Periphery EM sovereign spreads increased far more than Core EM spreads, with Periphery median spreads rising 45 bps to 332 bps since the Middle East conflict onset.
key_points: Core EMs (22 MSCI Index countries) doubled their share of global GDP from 14% (2000) to 33% (2025), while Periphery EMs (92 countries) remained stagnant at 6%
Core EMs reduced foreign currency borrowing exposure over 20 years by expanding domestic capital markets, while Periphery EMs maintained significantly elevated foreign currency exposure
Core EMs built larger foreign exchange reserve buffers, enabling greater capacity to absorb external shocks and allowing exchange rates to adjust
Core EMs experienced smaller inflation spikes during COVID and faster return to pre-pandemic levels compared to Periphery EMs
Periphery EM sovereign spreads increased by 45 bps from late February to 332 bps median since Middle East conflict onset, versus substantially smaller increases for Core EMs
Many Periphery economies face "fear of floating" and cannot deploy countercyclical policy during shocks due to inflation and financial stability concerns
why_it_matters: The analysis documents a structural divide in emerging market resilience that has direct implications for capital allocation, risk assessment, and portfolio positioning. Investors in EM-focused funds should distinguish between Core and Periphery exposures, as Periphery economies remain highly vulnerable to risk-off episodes. The findings also inform sovereign debt risk assessment and highlight which EM central banks have policy credibility to weather external shocks.
macro_context: The source examines how different emerging market economies respond to macro shocks including COVID, global monetary tightening in 2022, and the ongoing Middle East conflict. Core EMs with stronger buffers and credible monetary frameworks demonstrated resilience through lower spread widening and maintained ability to deploy countercyclical policy. Periphery EMs remain constrained by foreign currency debt exposure and limited reserve buffers.
important_numbers: Periphery EM median sovereign spread: 332 bps (post Middle East conflict)
Periphery EM spread increase: 45 bps since Feb 27
